channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Modi Urges Indians to Buy Less Gold and Skip Foreign Trips as Iran War Strains Economy
item.description: Under pressure from the economic fallout of the war in Iran and politically emboldened, Prime Minister Narendra Modi asks Indians to sacrifice.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Commuters on an electric bus in Delhi, India, last month. Mr. Modi appealed to people to conserve energy and use electric vehicles more.

item.title: Narges Mohammadi, Jailed Iranian Nobel Prize Winner Is Transferred to Tehran Hospital
item.description: Narges Mohammadi, a prominent human rights activist who has spent much of her adult life in and out of prison, will undergo treatment in Tehran after collapsing earlier this month, a foundation run by her family said.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg
item.media:credit: The Yomiuri Shimbun, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Narges Mohammadi, an Iranian human rights activist, last year. She received the Nobel Peace Prize in 2023 for her advocacy of women’s rights in her country.

item.title: How the U.S. Is Trying to Ensure the Dollar’s Dominance During Economic Turmoil
item.description: As the government has been devising plans to keep the dollar dominant, China has been making its own moves to increase global influence of the renminbi.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent has been leading the talks over the currency swaps.

item.title: As Trump Heads to Beijing, China Is ‘Locked and Loaded’ for a Fight
item.description: Beijing is signaling that it is ready for a trade showdown, and it is building up a legal arsenal in preparation.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Stevenson and Murphy Zhao
item.media:description: The port in Ningbo, China. “We are on the brink of a much more frequent or widespread use of Chinese countermeasures against U.S. sanctions,” said Andrew Gillholm, a China expert at Control Risks.

item.title: Trump to Be Joined by Elon Musk and Other CEOs at Xi Summit in China
item.description: The delegation includes business leaders across a wide range of industries, including Tim Cook of Apple and Elon Musk of Tesla.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager

item.title: A Single Infusion Could Suppress H.I.V. for Years, Study Suggests
item.description: A study of a few patients, to be presented this week, showed promise for a type of therapy that has already cured some blood cancers.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:description: A study led by an H.I.V. expert at the University of California, San Francisco, could offer a path toward a lasting H.I.V. treatment.

item.title: How Israel Turned Eurovision’s Stage Into a Soft Power Tool
item.description: Israel’s efforts to influence Eurovision’s vote were broader and started years earlier than previously known.
item.dc:creator: Mara Hvistendahl and Alex Marshall
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations

item.title: How to Win Eurovision With Just a Few Hundred Voters
item.description: Exclusive voting data shows that, despite Eurovision’s assurances, an Israeli campaign could easily have influenced last year’s contest.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Mara Hvistendahl
item.media:description: Yuval Raphael of Israel topped last year’s public vote and almost won Eurovision. Contest organizers don’t release detailed vote data.

item.title: The Top Restaurant Trends in New York City for 2026
item.description: Hip crustaceans, dessert trends and the never-ending reservation battle were among the things our chief critic took note of.
item.dc:creator: Ligaya Mishan

item.title: The Best New York City Neighborhoods for Restaurants
item.description: Great meals can be found in virtually any corner of the five boroughs, but here are some areas that stood out to our critic.
item.dc:creator: Ligaya Mishan
item.media:credit: Graham Dickie for The New York Times

item.title: A List of 100 Best Restaurants? For These Marathon Eaters, It’s a Dare.
item.description: As rankings proliferate, a cadre of completists have made it their mission to sample every single place on them.
item.dc:creator: Pete Wells
item.media:credit: Marissa Alper for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ms. Hunter with the chef Jean-Georges Vongerichten at his Jean-Georges restaurant in New York, which has three Michelin stars. Ms. Hunter tries to eat there once a month.

item.title: Man Charged With Assassination Attempt at Correspondents’ Dinner Pleads Not Guilty
item.description: Cole Tomas Allen faces four counts in what prosecutors say was a plot to kill President Trump and other top officials at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, and Jeanine Pirro, the U.S. attorney for the District of Columbia, at a news conference last month about the shooting. The defense lawyer asked that they be recused from the case.

item.title: They Were Promised New Septic Tanks. Trump Called It ‘Illegal DEI.’
item.description: The Justice Department ended a deal that had helped fund a solution to the sewage crisis in rural Alabama. “Almost like we are starting all over again,” one activist said.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pipe carrying sewage away from a home in Hayneville, Ala.

item.title: Drilling Into the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica
item.description: Ten people. Eight weeks. Three thousand feet to pierce a fast-melting Antarctic glacier.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Chang W. Lee
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Drilling equipment on the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica in January.

item.title: American Passengers Exposed to Hantavirus Begin Quarantine in U.S.
item.description: Eighteen people from a cruise ship that faced a hantavirus outbreak are being monitored at medical facilities in Nebraska and Georgia. One tested positive for the Andes virus, health officials said.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao, Jacey Fortin and Jin Yu Young
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Passengers being evacuated from the MV Hondius on Sunday.

item.title: Where Are the Passengers of the Hantavirus-Hit Cruise Ship Now?
item.description: Nearly two dozen countries are repatriating citizens who were aboard the MV Hondius, where three passengers died in a hantavirus outbreak.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: British citizens repatriated from the MV Hondius arriving at a hospital in northwest England on Sunday.

item.title: What to Know About the Hantavirus Outbreak on an Atlantic Cruise Ship
item.description: The health authorities are working to calm fears and contain the rare virus. Three passengers died after traveling on the ship, and several others fell ill or tested positive.
item.dc:creator: Amelia Nierenberg, Claire Moses and Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius stationed off the port of Praia, Cape Verde, on Sunday.

item.title: Accusations of Shenanigans Fly in Nebraska’s Senate Race
item.description: One Democrat is accused of being a Republican in disguise. Another plans to drop out to boost an independent. It’s primary eve in one of the country’s most unusual midterm contests.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Walker Pickering for The New York Times
item.media:description: The erosion of the Democratic Party brand in the Midwest and Great Plains has led to independent candidates’ sometimes mounting more credible campaigns in rural states like Nebraska.

item.title: Mike Lawler, N.Y.’s Most At-Risk House Republican, Is Accused of Self-Dealing
item.description: Several entities tied to Representative Mike Lawler, New York’s most endangered House Republican, paid a political consulting firm he once owned, raising ethics concerns.
item.dc:creator: Jeffery C. Mays and Grace Ashford
item.media:credit: Gregg Vigliotti for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Mike Lawler of New York is trying to win re-election this year in a race seen as a pickup opportunity for Democrats.

item.title: Inside the Elon Musk-OpenAI Trial Courtroom
item.description: The tech leaders, with combined net worths exceeding $670 billion, have brought props to court and traded icy stares as their legal dispute reaches a denouement.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Illustration by Alice Lagarde; Photographs by Brennan Smart for The New York Times, Jason Henry for The New York Times, Getty Images

item.title: Google Says Criminal Hackers Used A.I. to Find a Major Software Flaw
item.description: The company said that it had identified, for the first time, hackers using artificial intelligence to discover an unknown bug. The attempted attack represents “a taste of what’s to come,” one expert said.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz
item.media:description: “We have high confidence that the actor likely leveraged an A.I. model to support the discovery and weaponization of this vulnerability,” Google said in its report, published Monday.

item.title: Ukraine Looks to Step Away From Trump and U.S.
item.description: With peace talks on ice and Ukraine now more self-reliant, President Volodymyr Zelensky seems to be stepping away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Kim Barker, Constant Méheut and Maria Varenikova
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine in Yerevan, Armenia, last week. Mr. Zelensky has publicly criticized the United States in ways that would have been unthinkable last year.

item.title: Putin’s Forces Are Barely Inching Along on the Battlefield
item.description: The Russian military has yet to solve a fundamental problem: how to make big advances in eastern Ukraine when drones are everywhere.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne, Cassandra Vinograd and Milana Mazaeva
item.media:description: An Ukrainian soldier in the besieged eastern city of Kostiantynivka early this year.

item.title: NBC to Turn Wordle Into a TV Game Show, With Savannah Guthrie as Host
item.description: The word puzzle published by The New York Times is the inspiration for a game show that will begin airing next year. Jimmy Fallon is a producer.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum

item.title: Gisèle Pelicot’s Memoir Said Something Taboo About Victimhood. We Didn’t Listen.
item.description: A book by the world’s most famous survivor of sexual violence has been read as a manifesto or a cry of pain. What she wrote is far more complicated.
item.dc:creator: Parul Sehgal

item.title: The Fight to Euthanize Pablo Escobar’s Hippos in Colombia
item.description: Colombia is planning to cull a population of wild hippos, the offspring of the drug lord’s pets, dividing a town where hippos are the main draw.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Esteban Vanegas
item.media:credit: Esteban Vanegas for The New York Times
item.media:description: A male hippopotamus walking in Doradal, Colombia. Despite their bulk, they can easily outrun humans.

item.title: The Silence That Meets the Rape of Palestinians
item.description: Male and female Palestinians describe brutal sexual abuse at the hands of Israel’s prison guards, soldiers, settlers and interrogators.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
item.media:credit: Samar Hazboun for The New York Times
item.media:description: Suhaib Abualkebash.

item.title: Bill Cassidy Sold His Soul and Got Nothing in Return
item.description: A doctor and senator who voted to confirm Robert Kennedy still was snubbed by Trump in the Republican primary in Louisiana.
item.dc:creator: Gary Sernovitz

item.title: It’s All Connected
item.description: We look into the body’s circulatory systems.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton

item.title: Tell us Your Games Streak Story
item.description: We need your help for an upcoming article.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: First-Time Home Buyers Are Staying on the Sidelines as Costs Rise
item.description: The effects of the war in Iran have raised mortgage rates and lowered consumer confidence, making it even harder for house hunters.
item.dc:creator: Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sebastian Siadecki for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lillian Rouse and her fiancé, Mark Debney, in their rental home in North Carolina. Ms. Rouse said that delaying a home purchase had also meant putting off starting a family.

item.title: Kansas City, a Car Town, Spends Millions on World Cup Transit
item.description: Security and other expenses are piling up for host cities. In the smallest metro area staging games, the bill includes temporary bus systems in two states.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: David Robert Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Officials in Kansas City are working to ensure that a long-planned extension of the city’s streetcar system is completed ahead of the World Cup.

item.title: European Union Hits Israeli Settlers With Sanctions
item.description: Israel criticized the European Union’s announcement that it will apply sanctions to Israeli settlers over violence against Palestinians.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek, Aaron Boxerman and Koba Ryckewaert
item.media:description: Israeli settlers blocking a road as they try to stop Palestinian farmers from leaving their land after harvesting their olive trees in the village of Silwad, near East Ramallah, in October.

item.title: Starmer Says Defeat Hurt but Promises He’ll Fight to Stay On
item.description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer of Britain managed to quell an immediate mutiny in his Labour Party after last week’s election results. But for how long?
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer delivering a speech in London on Monday. “I get it. I feel it. And I take responsibility,” he said of last week’s results.
